title: Malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT1, blocking beta-oxidation
vignette: A research scientist studying the metabolic pathways that contribute to obesity feeds experimental animals a high-carbohydrate, high-protein diet for a prolonged period. A sample of liver tissue is then obtained from the animals, and the activity of various enzymes involved in fatty acid metabolism is measured and recorded. It is determined that beta-oxidation of fatty acids is inhibited within these cells as a result of the diet. An increase in which of the following substances is most likely responsible for the observed effect?

clues.text: metabolic pathways that contribute to obesity
clues.note: The framing is the well-fed/anabolic state, where the liver is synthesizing fat. In that state, fatty acid OXIDATION should be switched off - the question asks what molecule does the switching.

clues.text: high-carbohydrate, high-protein diet for a prolonged period
clues.note: A carbohydrate-rich, well-fed state raises insulin and citrate, driving de novo fatty acid synthesis. The same signals must simultaneously shut down fatty acid breakdown to avoid a futile cycle.

clues.text: beta-oxidation of fatty acids is inhibited
clues.note: Beta-oxidation occurs in the mitochondrial matrix; fatty acids enter only via the carnitine shuttle (carnitine palmitoyltransferase I / carnitine acyltransferase). The molecule that inhibits this shuttle is malonyl-CoA - the answer.

whatMatters: In the well-fed state, acetyl-CoA carboxylase makes MALONYL-CoA (the committed step of fatty acid synthesis). Malonyl-CoA also INHIBITS carnitine palmitoyltransferase I (carnitine acyltransferase), blocking fatty acid entry into mitochondria and thus inhibiting beta-oxidation. This prevents a futile cycle of simultaneous synthesis and breakdown. Answer D.
strategy: Find the molecule that DIRECTLY blocks the carnitine shuttle (CPT1), the rate-limiting entry into beta-oxidation. That is malonyl-CoA. Beware upstream/associated molecules: citrate drives synthesis (activates ACC, source of cytosolic acetyl-CoA) but doesn't directly inhibit oxidation; carnitine is REQUIRED for oxidation (more would help, not hinder); NADPH and acetoacetate belong to synthesis and ketone pathways.

eliminations.label: Acetoacetate - a ketone body (fasting), not a regulator here
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a ketone body produced from acetyl-CoA during starvation/fasting when oxaloacetate is scarce. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: ketogenesis is a FED-state opposite; acetoacetate does not inhibit the carnitine shuttle or beta-oxidation. ✅ Correct if: asked about fuel produced during prolonged fasting.

eliminations.label: Carnitine - required FOR beta-oxidation
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the carrier that shuttles long-chain fatty acids across the mitochondrial membrane for oxidation. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: carnitine ENABLES beta-oxidation, so increasing it would promote (not inhibit) oxidation; its deficiency impairs oxidation. ✅ Correct if: asked what is needed to transport fatty acids into mitochondria (or what's deficient in a carnitine-deficiency myopathy).

eliminations.label: Citrate - drives synthesis but doesn't directly block oxidation (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: in the fed state, mitochondrial citrate is exported and cleaved (ATP citrate lyase) to cytosolic acetyl-CoA, and citrate allosterically ACTIVATES acetyl-CoA carboxylase. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: citrate promotes fatty acid SYNTHESIS upstream, but it does not directly inhibit carnitine palmitoyltransferase I / beta-oxidation - malonyl-CoA does. The trap is stopping one step too early in the pathway. ✅ Correct if: asked what activates ACC or supplies cytosolic acetyl-CoA for synthesis.

eliminations.label: NADPH - reducing power for synthesis, not a regulator of oxidation
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the reducing equivalent used to build fatty acids (from the pentose phosphate pathway and malic enzyme). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: NADPH supplies electrons for synthesis but does not inhibit the carnitine shuttle or beta-oxidation. ✅ Correct if: asked what provides reducing power for fatty acid synthesis.

reasoning: Well-fed, high-carbohydrate state -> active de novo fatty acid synthesis in the liver
Acetyl-CoA carboxylase converts acetyl-CoA to malonyl-CoA (committed step of synthesis)
Malonyl-CoA also inhibits carnitine palmitoyltransferase I, blocking fatty acid entry into mitochondria
-> beta-oxidation is inhibited by increased malonyl-CoA; answer D
mechanism: In the well-fed state, abundant ATP and a high-carbohydrate load raise mitochondrial citrate, which is exported to the cytosol and cleaved by ATP citrate lyase to acetyl-CoA; insulin and citrate then activate acetyl-CoA carboxylase, which converts acetyl-CoA to MALONYL-CoA in the committed, rate-limiting step of de novo fatty acid synthesis. Malonyl-CoA additionally inhibits carnitine palmitoyltransferase I (carnitine acyltransferase), the transporter that shuttles long-chain fatty acids into the mitochondrial matrix where beta-oxidation occurs. By blocking this entry step, rising malonyl-CoA prevents the breakdown of newly synthesized fatty acids, avoiding a futile cycle of simultaneous synthesis and oxidation. Citrate and NADPH support synthesis upstream, and carnitine is required for oxidation, so none of them directly produce the observed inhibition.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Biochem > fatty acid metabolism: acetyl-CoA carboxylase (rate-limiting, makes malonyl-CoA; activated by citrate/insulin); malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT1 (carnitine shuttle) -> blocks beta-oxidation; reciprocal regulation prevents a futile cycle.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The stem asks what DIRECTLY inhibits beta-oxidation in the fed state. Trace to the carnitine shuttle (CPT1) and its inhibitor, malonyl-CoA - not an upstream synthesis substrate.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on malonyl-CoA, acetyl-CoA carboxylase, carnitine palmitoyltransferase I (CPT1)/carnitine shuttle, citrate shuttle, or futile cycle? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Citrate (your pick) drives synthesis and is upstream, so it feels causal - but it doesn't directly inhibit beta-oxidation; malonyl-CoA blocks CPT1. Carnitine is needed FOR oxidation, so increasing it wouldn't inhibit it.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT1 -> blocks beta-oxidation (fed state); citrate activates ACC -> synthesis; carnitine REQUIRED for oxidation; NADPH = reducing power for synthesis.

keywords.front: Molecule that inhibits beta-oxidation in the fed state
Committed/rate-limiting step of fatty acid synthesis
keywords.back: Acetyl-CoA -> malonyl-CoA by acetyl-CoA carboxylase (activated by citrate/insulin)
Transports long-chain fatty acids into mitochondria for beta-oxidation (CPT1/CPT2)
Exported to cytosol -> acetyl-CoA (ATP citrate lyase); activates ACC
Pentose phosphate pathway and malic enzyme
Ketone body of starvation/fasting (not fed-state regulation)

firstAid.detail: Biochem > acetyl-CoA carboxylase makes malonyl-CoA; malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT1, blocking beta-oxidation (reciprocal regulation).
firstAid.location: Biochem > acetyl-CoA carboxylase makes malonyl-CoA; malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT1, blocking beta-oxidation (reciprocal regulation).

firstAid.detail: Biochem > citrate (activates ACC), NADPH (reducing power), carnitine (shuttle for oxidation), acetoacetate (ketone).
firstAid.location: Biochem > citrate (activates ACC), NADPH (reducing power), carnitine (shuttle for oxidation), acetoacetate (ketone).

firstAid.detail: Biochem > fed: synthesis on, oxidation off (malonyl-CoA); fasting: oxidation/ketogenesis on.
firstAid.location: Biochem > fed: synthesis on, oxidation off (malonyl-CoA); fasting: oxidation/ketogenesis on.

memoryHook: Malonyl-CoA is the 'building' molecule that slams the mitochondrial door (CPT1) shut - you can't burn fat and build it at the same time.
